data.title: Indonesia’s flood death toll hits 883, hundreds still missing
data.description:
Indonesia’s disaster agency on Saturday reported a rise in the death toll from floods and landslides on the island of Sumatra, saying at least 883 people had been confirmed dead while more than 500 were missing.
The National Disaster Management Agency (BNPB) said the figures covered three provinces – Aceh, North Sumatra and West Sumatra – which had been hit by days of intense rainfall that triggered overflowing rivers, landslides and severe damage to transport infrastructure.
The agency said 520 people were still unaccounted for and around 4,200 had been injured.
A chain of tropical storms and monsoonal rains has pummelled Southeast and South Asia in recent days, triggering landslides and flash floods from the Sumatran rainforest to the highland plantations of Sri Lanka.

Some 1,770 people have been killed in natural disasters unfolding across Indonesia, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam since last week.
In Indonesia, Aceh province recorded the highest number of fatalities, with 345 deaths and 174 people listed as missing. BNPB said many of the injured were from rural districts on steep terrain prone to landslides.
In North Sumatra, officials confirmed 312 deaths and 133 missing, while West Sumatra reported 226 fatalities and 213 missing.
The agency warned that the number of victims could rise further as rescue teams reached isolated areas.

Access has been hampered by dozens of damaged bridges and blocked roads, leaving some villages without electricity, clean water or telecommunications for days.
Environmental organisations have linked the scale of destruction to rapid deforestation and hillside development in parts of Sumatra, arguing that weakened watersheds have made communities more vulnerable to heavy rainfall.
Indonesia frequently experiences floods and landslides during monsoon season, but experts say climate change is contributing to more extreme weather events across the region.
The government has deployed the military, police and additional disaster response personnel to the affected provinces.
Further heavy rain threatened Indonesia’s flood-ravaged island of Sumatra on Saturday as the governor of one hard-hit province warned that the death toll could climb beyond 883 because of starvation.


Indonesia’s national weather agency said rain could return on Saturday to the provinces of Aceh and North Sumatra. Aceh Governor Muzakir Manaf said response teams were still searching for bodies in “waist-deep” mud.
However, starvation was one of the gravest threats now hanging over remote and inaccessible villages.
“Many people need basic necessities. Many areas remain untouched in the remote areas of Aceh,” he told reporters. “People are not dying from the flood, but from starvation. That’s how it is.”
Entire villages had been washed away in the rainforest-cloaked Aceh Tamiang region, Muzakir said.
People are not dying from the flood, but from starvation. That’s how it is
Muzakir Manaf, governor of Indonesia’s Aceh province
“The Aceh Tamiang region is completely destroyed, from the top to the bottom, down to the roads and down to the sea,” he said. “Many villages and subdistricts are now just names.”
Aceh resident Munawar Liza Zainal said he felt “betrayed” by the Indonesian government, which had so far shrugged off pressure to declare a national disaster.
“This is an extraordinary disaster that must be faced with extraordinary measures,” he said, echoing frustrations voiced by other flood victims. “If national disaster status is only declared later, what’s the point?”
Declaring a national disaster would free up resources and help government agencies coordinate their response.

Analysts have suggested Indonesia could be reluctant to declare a disaster and seek additional foreign aid because it would show it was not up to the task.
Indonesia’s government this week insisted it could handle the fallout.
The scale of devastation has only just become clear in other parts of Sumatra as engorged rivers shrink and floodwaters recede.
Humanitarian groups fear that the scale of calamity could be without precedent, even for a nation prone to natural disasters.
Sri Lanka’s death toll jumped by more than 100 on Friday to 607, as the government warned that fresh rains raised the risk of new landslides.
Thailand has reported 276 deaths and Malaysia two, while at least two people were killed in Vietnam after heavy rains triggered a series of landslides.

data.title: Jakarta vs the internet: Indonesia’s gamble with digital freedom
data.description:
What happens when a democracy begins to fear its own internet? In Indonesia, that question is no longer hypothetical, as the government threatens to pull the plug on major online platforms many of its people rely upon.
When Jakarta warned last month that it could block major platforms like Cloudflare and Wikipedia, the move was justified as a crackdown on online gambling – a vice officially outlawed in the world’s largest Muslim-majority nation.
But for tech industry players and free-speech advocates, the move was something else entirely: another step in the state’s steady expansion of power over the digital realm.
The order came on November 17 from the Ministry of Communication and Digital Affairs, which demanded that 25 global companies – among them Cloudflare, Wikipedia, ChatGPT, Duolingo, Dropbox and Getty Images – comply with a registration requirement introduced under a 2020 ministerial regulation.

In theory, the rule helps authorities track and manage illicit online activity. In practice, critics say it hands sweeping powers to the government to decide which platforms stay visible and which may vanish overnight from Indonesia’s internet.
Officials singled out Cloudflare, an American digital infrastructure provider that protects websites from cyberattacks, as a key offender. The ministry said its services had been used by thousands of gambling websites, accounting for more than three-quarters of the 10,000 domains it blocked this year.
“The majority of online gambling sites hide behind Cloudflare,” Alexander Sabar, director general of digital supervision at the ministry, told reporters on November 19. “They know Cloudflare provides strong protection, making it difficult to take them down simply by blocking [them].”
Not only had Cloudflare not registered, it had no official representative or server in Indonesia, Sabar said.
“Our request is simple. Cloudflare must filter and no longer accept service requests from sites that are clearly detrimental to the Indonesian public, particularly online gambling sites,” he added.

Indonesia’s regulation obliges every online entity, from massive global platforms to small blogs, to register with the authorities, theoretically placing more than 1.8 billion websites under potential state oversight. Rights groups describe the rule as impossible to enforce and ripe for abuse.
“Mandating registration for all without exception is an impossible regulation to enforce and ineffective in combating the spread of illegal and harmful content in the digital space,” the Coalition for Democratisation and Moderation of Indonesian Digital Space said in a statement on November 21.
The regulation “contains articles that are open to multiple interpretations and is a threat to privacy”, it added.
‘Burning the barn’
Indonesia has long been pushing for “digital sovereignty”, an ethos that prioritises controlling digital infrastructure, data and technology to ensure it benefits the nation and is not solely dependent on foreign influence.
Such policies mirror trends across Southeast Asia, where governments increasingly frame online regulation as necessary protection against disinformation, extremism and gambling. But digital innovation advocates warn that Indonesia’s model is blurring the line between law enforcement and censorship.
“Digital platforms’ jurisdiction over their content, policies and user data practices are being undermined by this regulation,” said Hafiz Noer, head of Gadjah Mada University’s Centre for Digital Society.

The rules gave Indonesia’s government “the authority to take down content that is deemed disturbing to the public”, he said. “This is prone to being used for political purposes.”
Jakarta has blocked internet services before. In 2022, it briefly cut off access to PayPal and the gaming platform Steam after they failed to register on time, triggering a nationwide backlash.
“We can’t imagine what would happen if Cloudflare was blocked. The impact on society would be severe,” said Hafizh Nabiyyin of the Southeast Asia Freedom of Expression Network, noting that countless government and media websites rely on the service.
“It’s like the government wants to kill rats by burning down the rice barn.”
The real threat
Cloudflare officials met ministry representatives late last month, pledging to study the regulation further and build reporting channels for content moderation, Sabar said in a statement on November 26.
But the stand-off has left ordinary Indonesians uneasy about the arbitrariness of state action and the future of their digital access.
“Wikipedia, Duolingo and ChatGPT are not a threat,” wrote one user on social media. “What is a threat is the state’s inability to crack down on online gambling!”
Transparency advocates agree that the core problem is not foreign platforms, but domestic accountability.
Investigations have found that these gambling sites involve officials
Hafizh Nabiyyin, digital rights campaigner
“Investigations have found that these gambling sites involve officials,” Hafizh said. “This is what must be dealt with firmly. Law enforcement must take firm action against officials who provide [backing] for these gambling businesses.”
Aribowo Sasmito, co-founder of the anti-misinformation group Mafindo, said it was reasonable for authorities to seek greater transparency from tech firms but argued that complex registration rules were keeping many from complying.
“The internet is quite difficult to regulate,” Aribowo said. “I’m sure they don’t mean to intentionally not register.”
Proponents of tougher rules argue that the state has a duty to protect Indonesia’s more than 220 million internet users from harmful or illegal content.
But critics fear that good intentions have become a gateway to unchecked power, with Indonesia’s digital future set to be shaped by the contest between control and openness for years to come.

data.title: US actress Tara Reid says she was drugged. Surveillance footage shows her downing wine
data.description:
Newly released surveillance footage from the US hotel where American actress Tara Reid claims she was drugged last month shows the actress downing more drinks than she recalls.
Reid, 50, maintains she was drugged on November 23 at the DoubleTree Hotel by the Chicago O’Hare Airport, insisting she had just one drink before being wheeled out on a stretcher and taken to the hospital.
Hotel security video, published by TMZ on Friday, shows a bartender bringing a glass of liquid to the American Pie actress, who is wearing a coat and beanie. Reid then pours the contents, which look like white wine, into a wine glass and drinks it.
A different bartender then brings over another refill, as Reid chats up other customers. She then pours that into her wine glass, which she takes with her as she packs up her things and says goodbye.
Another clip shows Reid – no longer wearing the coat, but still wearing the beanie – on the other side of the bar, drinking from a wine glass as she talks to a man, who TMZ identified as YouTuber Sean P.
Reid allegedly named the internet personality in her police report, accusing him of slipping something into her drink. He has denied her allegation.
The surveillance video was published on the heels of Rosemont authorities telling local outlet WFLD that footage they reviewed did not show anyone tampering with Reid’s drinks. They contended that the footage only shows a bartender covering a glass that Reid left behind when she went outside to smoke.
Cops are awaiting hospital records and potential chemical testing results.
Reid insisted to TMZ on Wednesday that she only recalled “having one drink and waking up in the hospital the next day without remembering anything”.

data.title: Wicked songs in Cantonese? New online series champions Hong Kong talent
data.description:
Coinciding with the recent release of Wicked: For Good, a Hong Kong creative team has also brought Oz to life – in Cantonese.
“Wicked in Canto”, a series of videos featuring songs from the global musical phenomenon performed in the city’s mother tongue, is making waves on YouTube and Instagram, with tracks dropping weekly.
The project champions Hong Kong talent while highlighting the potential of international musical theatre productions performed in Cantonese.
Officially leading the project’s adaptation of the lyrics is Jason Lung, a University of Hong Kong graduate in Chinese literature and translation, who was inspired while listening to the soundtrack of the first Wicked film.
“I just kept thinking, these songs sound like they would be really good in Cantonese,” he recalls. “There’s a certain musicality and emotional depth in our language that felt like a perfect match.”


Lung reached out to Marco Tang, a seasoned performer from New Gen, a Hong Kong troupe of rising young performers, and the project quickly took flight.
“Energy flows both ways,” Tang says. “By giving these incredible new artists this platform, we’re not only promoting musical theatre in Hong Kong but also giving back to the creative community.
“They lift the art form, and the art form lifts them.”
What followed was the formation of a 17-person troupe comprising fellow industry experts and friends, including a coalition of performers, vocal coaches and even a make-up artist, all united by a common goal.
“This isn’t just about covering songs – it’s about building something for Hong Kong,” Lung explains. “The professional musical scene here is relatively small. We have the talent, but we often lack the large-scale, visible platforms. ‘Wicked in Canto’ is that opportunity to push musical theatre here.”

The project is a remarkable act of artistic devotion, funded entirely from the team’s own pockets.
“We are completely non-profit,” Lung says. “Every hour in the recording studio, every bit of filming – it’s all come from us.”
He adds that the team reached out to Music Theatre International in New York, which licenses the musical, for permission to riff on the works.
“I wasn’t even really expecting a reply, but they gave us the green light almost as soon as we sent the email,” he says.
With an ensemble of eight performers playing the main characters – Elphaba, Glinda, Fiyero, Nessarose, Boq, Madame Morrible, the Wizard and Doctor Dillamond – the casting process was an exercise in precision.
“It was crucial to match people to their singing styles and personalities,” vocal coach Alex Kwok says. “Glinda’s songs are sometimes basically what you would hear in opera, whereas Elphaba needs that tiny little bit of rock-genre edge in her voice.”

Tang adds that they paired performers with roles based on more than just vocal range or technique, sharing that they cast actress and singing coach Hailey Mak Hiu-lam – “someone who is very sweet and bubbly in real life” – to play the role of Glinda, for example.
“We didn’t just want singers – we wanted embodiments,” he says.
Hong Kong Drama Awards nominee Chloe Wong, who plays Elphaba in the series, emphasises the significance of the original Wicked stage musical when it debuted in the US in 2003, saying it was the first of its kind to incorporate such a wide variety of musical styles in a single production.
“[Wicked] wasn’t just ‘songs’; it was a complete, genre-blending universe that gave each character a distinct musical soul and mirrored the clash of their worlds,” Wong says. “That revolutionary approach is a big part of why it has endured and why it translates so powerfully.”
With such strong source material, the linguistic journey was one of the project’s greatest challenges. Starting in January this year – two months after the release of the first Wicked film – and working tirelessly to finish the translations by September, Lung wrestled for months with Stephen Schwartz’s lyrics.

Not only did he translate the specific vowels in each song to match the rhymes used melodically, but he also had to somehow carry over culturally specific idioms, humour and Oz-specific wordplay for a Cantonese-speaking audience.
Lung says that creative compromise was the key, highlighting the example of one simple word: blonde.
“That word in ‘What Is This Feeling’ was extremely hard to translate for me. You see, in the original, ‘blonde’ is this loaded cultural insult. It’s not just a hair colour; it’s a stereotype. It’s basically calling someone dumb in a Western way, right?
“But, in Cantonese, and in our cultural context, that specific meaning simply doesn’t exist. We don’t have that same historical or social baggage attached to hair colour. If I just translated it literally as ‘golden hair’, it would be meaningless and confusing.
“So, I had to deconstruct the word to its core function in the song. It’s not about the hair; it’s about what the hair represents to Elphaba at that moment. I had to find a Cantonese concept that could land with the same ironic punch. That is the approach I took when I translated the soundtrack.
“I guess you’ll have to watch our video to find out what we ended up going with!”

Looking beyond Broadway for inspiration, the team found a blueprint in East Asia, specifically in Korea.
“We are incredibly inspired by Korean musical productions, which really took off recently,” Kwok says.
“Their advances in musical theatre, the professionalism of their adaptations and translations – they show that localised musicals can be world-class. They prove this model can work and thrive culturally, and I really hope that the same can happen in Hong Kong.”

data.title: Drones buzz French nuclear base amid Russian hybrid warfare fears
data.description:
The French military used jammers as several suspected drones flew over a closely guarded base housing nuclear ballistic submarines, prosecutors said on Friday.
It was the latest in a series of mysterious drone flights over airports and sensitive military and industrial sites across Europe.
Three-and-a-half years into Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, there is growing concern that such disruption could be part of hybrid war tactics by Moscow against the European Union, which has backed Kyiv.
No drones were shot down and no pilots were identified during the flyover on Thursday evening at the submarine base on Ile Longue, a peninsula off the Brittany coast in northwestern France, prosecutors said as they opened an investigation.


“No link with foreign interference” had been established at this stage, prosecutor Frederic Teillet said.
The investigation had to “confirm whether or not these were drones” and determine “the type and number of devices,” he added.
One source close to the case said that five drones had been detected above the base at around 6.30pm on Thursday.
An anti-drone and search operation was launched, and the marine battalion, which protects the base, fired several anti-drone shots, the source added.

“The marines fired a jammer, not a firearm,” Teillet said.
The Ile Longue base is home to France’s four ballistic missile submarines – Le Triomphant, Le Temeraire, Le Vigilant and Le Terrible.
At least one is permanently at sea to ensure nuclear deterrence.
“Sensitive infrastructure was not threatened,” said Guillaume Le Rasle, a spokesman for the maritime prefecture.
Speaking earlier in the day, he said that it was “too early to determine” the origin of the drones, adding however that the flights had been “intended to cause concern among the population”.


Defence Minister Catherine Vautrin told French broadcaster TF1 that any overflight of a military base was prohibited in France.
Praising the response of the military personnel at the base, she added: “A complaint has been filed, an investigation is under way, and it is this investigation that will determine what this overflight was all about.”
France and the United Kingdom are the only European countries other than Russia with nuclear weapons.
France has maintained a sea-based nuclear deterrent force since 1971. Its ballistic missile submarines are equipped with M51 strategic missiles with multiple nuclear warheads.
The Ile Longue base is a closely guarded site, employing 2,000 people including 1,500 civilians. It is protected by 120 maritime police officers in coordination with marines.
Drone flights are prohibited over the Crozon peninsula, which includes Ile Longue, to protect the military infrastructure located there.
However, drone flights in the restricted area were not unprecedented, Le Rasle said.

data.title: November’s hottest watch moments, from Lady Becks’ Patek Philippe to Dubai Watch Week and GPHG winners
data.description:
November was packed with high horology moments the world over. In Geneva, the Grand Prix d’Horlogerie de Genève gathered once again to honour the year’s best watches, while in Dubai, retailer Ahmed Seddiqi & Sons organised the most well-attended Dubai Watch Week yet.
In the UK, the newly minted Lady Victoria Beckham sported a stunning watch to complement the outfits she designed for herself and her husband – now Sir David Beckham – at the latter’s knighthood investiture.
And brands like Vacheron Constantin, Jaeger-LeCoultre, Czapek and Omega released major updates to their collections, while LVMH partnered with La Joux-Perret by purchasing a minority stake to produce even more cutting-edge watch movements.
Dubai Watch Week was bigger and better than ever

Dubai Watch Week returned to the Middle East, where over 90 brands exhibited new releases to some 49,000 visitors from November 19 to 23. Watch professionals, enthusiasts, collectors, artisans and more converged on an all-new site, Burj Park, one of Dubai’s green spaces, for what many are calling one of the best watch fairs of the year – a huge claim considering the fair is organised by just one retailer, Ahmed Seddiqi & Sons.

What sets this year’s Dubai Watch Week apart from previous editions are the sheer amount of gobstopping releases from major brands saved specifically for this occasion. Chopard unveiled the Grand Strike, a new masterpiece from its L.U.C. collection, while sci-fi watchmakers Urwerk and Ulysse Nardin partnered to release the UR-Freak. Meanwhile, Atelier Wen collaborated with Revolution Watch to debut a new type of finishing for its latest collection: the Ancestra Yao features enamelling from Chinese master Kong Linjun in addition to a gratté dial.

Brands like Tudor and H. Moser & Cie leaned heavily into the desert surrounds of the fair for their releases. Tudor launched a new addition to its Ranger collection featuring a Dune White dial, as well as a small-wrist-friendly 36mm iteration that’s one of the year’s sleeper hits from the brand. Not to be outdone, H. Moser & Cie released a compelling Streamliner Perpetual Moon Concept Meteorite that features a meteorite dial and a gold-toned fume effect finishing.

Girard Perregaux, Bremont, Bell & Ross also rolled out their own hits. The former released the Laureato Three Gold Bridges, a model that sees its famous three gold bridges movement layout in its popular sports watch configuration, while Bremont released its most daring version of the Terra Nova Jumping Hour yet with an aventurine dial. Bell & Ross made full use of its signature square dial to showcase a tourbillon and a micro-rotor in the new BRX3 Tourbillon Micro Rotor.
These are but a fraction of the stunning releases from all the brands at Dubai Watch Week, but if this year’s fair is anything to go by, collectors will be doubly spoiled in autumn watch seasons to come.
High art for high watchmaking

Away from Dubai Watch Week, horology brands were equally busy releasing elaborate, high complication pieces. Atelier Wen debuted a new integrated bracelet watch called the Inflection, pushing the boundaries of tantalum, a notoriously hard metal to machine at all, let alone for a full watch and bracelet set.

Meanwhile, Richemont-owned high horology maisons Vacheron Constantin and Jaeger-LeCoultre doubled down on the artistry of watchmaking. The former focused on a set of astronomy-inspired creations for its Les Cabinotiers line, including an “armillary tourbillon”, a biaxial, spherical tourbillon inspired by the Greek myth of the Pleiades (the seven daughters of Atlas and Pleione who were turned into stars), and two double-sided grand complication watches inspired by the moon, Ptolemy and Copernicus. Jaeger-LeCoultre went all out on the Reverso Hybris Artistica Calibre 179, which features a gyrotourbillon with 123 components and meticulously decorated plates, bridges and dials.

Grand Seiko and Czapek also aren’t backing down when it comes to releasing wearable art. The former released the Elegance GMT SBGM255 Snowdrop and SBGM 257 Moondrop – two colourways of a sector-dialled GMT, one a stark white with deep blue GMT hand and the other featuring a navy dial with a gold GMT hand and accents. Czapek celebrated its 10th anniversary as Czapek & Cie with the Time Jumper, which uses two discs to display time via a jump hour complication. For those worried about a minimal dial surface, the case can be opened to expose the beautiful Calibre 10 within.

Rounding out this month’s major releases is Omega, which updated the Seamaster Planet Ocean as the watch model turns 20. This new fourth generation iteration updates the case architecture, which features more aggressive angles, and removes the helium escape valve, which is ordinarily signature to Omega’s dive watches.
GPHG 2025: and the winner is …

The 2025 edition of the Watch Oscars came to a resounding close as the winners of this year’s edition of the Grand Prix d’Horlogerie de Genève (GPHG) were announced in Geneva on November 13. Nineteen watches, brands or industry icons, selected by a jury of GPHG Academy members led by GPHG president Nick Foulkes, took home a prize out of a total of 90 nominated timepieces.

Breguet took home the Aiguille d’Or – or watch of the year – for its one-handed, minimalist Classique Souscription 2025, while Greubel Forsey’s hyper-complicated Nano Foudroyante took home the Mechanical Exception Watch Prize. Chopard took home the most awards for one brand, winning the Sports Watch Prize for its Alpine Eagle 41 SL Cadence 8HF and the Ladies’ Complication Prize for its Imperiale Four Seasons.

Watchmaker Kari Voutilainen also took home two awards this year for different brands. His UJ-2 created for Urban Jürgensen took home the Men’s Watch Prize, while the Voutilainen 28GML Souyou won the Artistic Crafts Watch Prize. Chongqing-based Fam Al Hut took home the Audacity Prize for its Möbius Mark 1, which houses a biaxial tourbillon, retrograde minutes and jumping hour complications in a 42mm oval case.
Watch moments for Sir and Lady Becks, Mark Wahlberg and Jeremy Allen White

If ever there was a special occasion that warranted an extraordinary watch, it was Sir David Beckham’s investiture at Windsor Castle, where the ex-football star was knighted by King Charles. Beckham’s wife – now styled Lady Victoria or Lady Beckham, thank you very much – made sure the man of the house looked his best with the first menswear suit from her fashion brand Victoria Beckham, while the lady herself sported an appropriately dazzling Patek Philippe Gondolo Haute Joaillerie.
Meanwhile, amid the glitz and glamour of the Formula One Las Vegas Grand Prix in late November, Mark Wahlberg re-established himself as watch collecting royalty as he eschewed his array of Pateks, Rolexes and other high horology heavy-hitters for his Timex Ironman 100-Lap. The cult-classic digital Timex is durable, casual, affordable and, as Wahlberg proved, perfectly at home next to the racing track.

On the red carpet, stars gathered for the Governors Awards, which recognises sustained filmmaking and lifetime achievements (not to be confused with the Governors Ball, the famous after-party for the Oscars in February). Omega, Cartier, Richard Mille, Chopard and more dominated the red carpet, but for us, the most interesting wears were arguably Leonardo DiCaprio very discreetly sporting a Rolex Land-Dweller, and Jeremy Allen White pairing his custom Louis Vuitton tuxedo with the recently revisited Monterey watch.
LVMH buys a minority stake in La Joux-Perret

Luxury French conglomerate LVMH added more components to its watch division as it acquired a minority stake in La Joux-Perret, which is owned by competing watch conglomerate Citizen Group. La Joux-Perret specialises in making durable, high-end movements for production watch models. The G100 calibre boasts a 28,800 vph (4hz) beat rate with 68 hours of power reserve, more than the ETA 2824 or Sellita SW200, which the G100 is often compared to.
Both companies have a commercial agreement to focus on innovation and design for movements for LVMH’s brands like Hublot, Tag Heuer and Zenith. La Joux-Perret is still fully independent and free to produce movements for its current client base. Previously, La Joux-Perret and LVMH collaborated on solar quartz movements for Tag Heuer, and on movements for Tiffany & Co.

data.title: Your horoscopes for December 2025, the month of the Earth Rat: work, money and health
data.description:
It’s December 2025, marking the Rat Month from December 7 to January 4. The elements of the month are Yang Earth and Water. The Earth Rat, wu zi, represents the 11th and penultimate month of the Chinese calendar.
The energy of 2025 asked for a letting-go attitude; 2026 asks you to rise and shine with an encouraging, renewed perspective.

In traditional Chinese culture, the energy of those born under the sign of the Rat (1948, 1960, 1972, 1984, 1996, 2008 and 2020) is highly playful and active, especially late in the evening, when they become alert, attentive and highly creative.

The Horse is the nemesis of the Rat, so be warned that 2026 will be an important astrological year for you, with plenty of movement and change. These two signs will have to work hard to make progress together, finding it difficult to agree and find harmony.
How Rat-like are you?

Those born during the Rat Month (December) possess great charm and diligence and are chatty, intelligent and always eager to make friends; the Rat energy draws people to them with ease. There’s something about the Rat's nature that generates trust, making their friendships long-lasting and genuine.
Those born on a Rat Day find that looking good is important, and they are always well-groomed; they believe outward appearance matters. Instinctively, they understand that you only have one chance to make a first impression, taking care never to be caught off guard looking at anything less than perfect.
Those born in the Rat Hours (11.00pm-1.00am) have a disposition for nocturnal occupations. Some may find them distant and secretive, but also wise. They are always in demand for an opinion.
Ideal career choices for Rats include: motivational speaker, personal coach, antiques dealer, estate agent and stockbroker. They may also be interested in anything related to transport, shipping, import-export and financial institutions.

Famous Rats include King Charles, Prince Harry, Bono, Mark Zuckerberg, Andrew Lloyd Webber, Cristiano Ronaldo, Avril Lavigne, Katy Perry, Dwayne Johnson, Scarlett Johansson and William Shakespeare.
What does December hold for your astrological sign?
These generic forecasts carry different nuances depending upon whether this animal rules your Year, Month, Day or Hour.
KEY:
√ Lucky
∞ Neutral Luck
X Not so lucky.
√ Rat – choose your words carefully

This is your astrological month with the potential for gossip and conflict. Keep your interactions with others cordial and positive, and avoid getting involved in any unnecessary drama. Travel improves your luck as broadening cultural horizons can both stimulate and distract from mundane daily tasks. Be careful of water-related injuries, especially on slippery surfaces.
√ √ Ox – your luckiest month of the year

As the Rat is your astrological friend, you may look forward to positive career prospects and a boost to your finances. This may be considered your luckiest month of the year! You gain good rewards and recognition, with the potential for a substantial end of year bonus. Channel your creative energy, so if you have been considering taking up a hobby, this is a great time for you to express yourself through art or the written word. Take your time before making any decisions as you head towards 2026.
∞ Tiger – beware of weather conditions

After a busy year, you may be considering a festive overseas trip or looking forward to family from overseas visiting to strengthen bonds. Broadening your horizons and enjoying a different environment may be a pleasing distraction from work-related commitments. Do beware of weather conditions if you are heading into unpredictable climates to ensure safety and wellbeing. Staying mobile will help you address any challenges you may be facing, especially if you have been involved in large-scale projects. If you have any new ideas pending, it would be advisable to wait until you have entered the New Year to execute any of them.
∞ Rabbit – focus on your career prospects

You can finally stick your head out of your burrow with a sigh of relief after a busy few months. Your fortune appears to improve with less delay and setbacks. Persevere and focus on your career prospects, with rewards and positive recognition beckoning after a year of hard work. Determination may finally pay off. To be able to enjoy all that may become available to you, ensure you have enough rest and nutritious meals to keep insomnia and stomach-related issues at bay.
∞ Dragon – your general health appears strong

Although the Rat has your back in many ways, you appear to be prone to accidents and injuries, so avoid overstretching or high-impact workouts. Your general health appears strong and stable. Team effort is key to success, as you may enjoy rewards and recognition. The future looks bright.
√ Snake – look forward to a smooth sailing month

After your astrological clash month of November, you may look forward to a month of smooth sailing, with the potential for some unexpected financial gains. However, try not to be tempted into any bullish investments as they may fall short of your expectations. You appear to be confident and able to overcome any challenges with ease and confidence. Be careful when using anything sharp or hiking on any rough terrain.
XX Horse – avoid high-risk financial dealings

It’s your astrological clash month, so aim to adopt a calm, adaptable attitude, which may prove challenging in many ways. Proceed with caution to avoid high-risk financial dealings. You are prone to being scammed as the cyberworld may be unpredictable with desperate people short on cash creating chaos for many during the distractions of the festive season. Falling for any scam could lead to personal and financial loss, resulting in family disharmony.
∞ Sheep – be careful when driving

Expenditure may be high at this time of year, obviously, but also due to some unexpected bills that may have been overlooked. The good news is that you may uncover ways to make up some gains to earn an unexpected second income, helping to ease financial pressure at this most expensive time of the year. Be careful when driving during the festive season, being observant of others’ lack of attention.
√ Monkey – maintain a humble attitude

You appear to be on a financial roll this month, easing pressure during this expensive season as your wealth luck appears to flourish. If so, put some amount away for that rainy day when you will be so grateful to yourself to have some financial insurance. Be kind and considerate to those you come into contact with as this will add to your karmic bank. Maintaining a humble and open-minded attitude is key to learning and personal growth. If you are doing any strengthening exercises, be patient with yourself; take time to warm up to prevent muscle fatigue.
∞ Rooster – beware of mindless gossip

You love the festive season and come into your element with social activity; however, do take care not to burn out after a rocking time as you may be feeling rather overwhelmed. The good news is that you have plenty of support. Beware of mindless gossip about loved ones, avoid impulsive action and hold back your reactive nature this month to prevent awkward moments and minor frustrations. Pay close attention to your health, as your immune system may be weaker than usual. Keep active as you may feel stiffness of the limbs, resulting in fatigue.
√ Dog – gain some potential personal exposure

In addition to enjoying the lead up to the festive season, you may be brimming over with creativity. This is an excellent month to consider your early New Year plans and to develop new strategies for potential new ventures for 2026. Use social platforms for networking, making new contacts and gaining some potential personal exposure.
X Pig – practice patience and understanding

Beware of conflict and awkward moments due to some third-party interference and misunderstanding. Be mindful of giving too much information away, as walls have ears and there may be some spies close by. Practice patience and understanding to avoid unnecessary stress and conflict. Take regular breaks during the day to maintain a sharp mindset to manage tedious tasks.

data.title: China hiker wears clothing tag to help find missing children, expand search, raise awareness
data.description:
A woman from northeastern China, who faced online harassment for wearing a clothing tag while hiking, later clarified that it contained information about a missing child as part of a brand’s charitable initiative.
Deng, hailing from Jilin province, recently posted a hiking video in which the tag on her backpack was visible, quickly inciting criticism across mainland social media.
One user commented: “She’s probably one of those who buys something, wears it once, and then returns it for a refund. How embarrassing!”
“She is so vain. If she can’t afford it, she shouldn’t pretend. It’s people like her who make business difficult,” remarked another.

Some even revisited a video that Deng shared last year, highlighting that her ski suit had the same tag attached during that occasion.
In China, e-commerce platforms usually provide a seven-day unconditional return policy to protect consumers. However, some individuals exploit this policy, transforming online stores into their personal free wardrobes.
During this year’s November 11, known as the Double 11 shopping festival, some retailers implemented preventive measures against exploitative returns by affixing oversized cloth tags or using combination locks on garment zippers.
Deng felt unjustly targeted by the wave of criticism, with many comments addressing her appearance and even resorting to regional discrimination.
In a follow-up video, she clarified that the backpack was bought in September and the ski suit in 2023, both of which fell outside the return window.

Deng also disclosed that the tags contained information about missing children to raise awareness, which she found significant, and that was why she kept them.
She displayed one of the tags, which included details about a missing boy with the surname Li, featuring his photo, personal information, and his parents’ contact number.
Shortly thereafter, the head of the clothing brand NO1NW, surnamed Meng, publicly expressed gratitude to Deng.
Meng informed Jimu News that the tags featuring information about missing children were part of a public welfare project that the company had been running for four years.

When the brand was first launched, they printed tags based on public information provided by organisations dedicated to locating missing people, updating the details every one to two years.
Meng noted that customers who received clothing with these tags could see the information and take the tags with them while hiking, thereby expanding the search for missing children.
“Greater dissemination brings more hope,” Meng added.
In recognition of Deng’s support, NO1NW presented her with a set of ski clothing, a hat, and several detachable tags with details of missing children.
Deng shared that she received apologies from some online critics and expressed her intention to continue hiking and skiing with the tags.

“I hope people will approach situations more rationally in the future. Don’t attack individuals without evidence,” she remarked.
The incident swiftly garnered widespread attention on mainland social media, with related posts amassing over 8 million views.
One observer commented: “Clothing tags are not moral shackles. There should be less prejudice and more trust and compassion among people.”
Estimates regarding the number of trafficked children in China annually vary widely, ranging from the official government figure of around 10,000 to independent assessments as high as 200,000. The precise number remains challenging to ascertain due to a lack of complete and transparent data.

data.title: AI is already taking jobs in Southeast Asia. Next comes a ‘great divergence’, UN warns
data.description:
For Malaysian tech worker Sam, there were three short steps to being replaced by robots at a global social media company’s Kuala Lumpur office.
First came weeks spent training the company’s artificial intelligence systems. Then came the ominous warnings of “restructuring”. Finally, the inevitable: redundancy.
“One day, out of nowhere, we had to attend a town hall,” said the 29-year-old, who asked to use a pseudonym to protect his job prospects.
“They never explicitly said it was due to AI, but we all knew the direction they were heading.”

Across Southeast Asia, millions face a similar threat as AI reshapes companies’ business models, erases old roles and creates new ones in what experts warn could be an upheaval without modern parallel.
A new report from the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) on the technology’s impact in the Asia-Pacific warns that while the region manufactures one-third of the chips driving the AI boom, it is also home to some of the world’s most unequal societies – amplifying the risk of millions being left behind.
The study, titled “The Next Great Divergence: Why AI May Widen Inequality Between Countries”, says that the progress made over centuries in narrowing global wealth gaps through trade and development could be undone in a single technological generation.
“Is AI going to be the great equaliser or a great amplifier of global inequality?” asked Philip Schellekens, chief economist for the UNDP Asia-Pacific Regional Bureau.
He urged governments and tech companies to “above all, put people first” in the AI race.

AI “enters a world where inequality between countries – and within them – is massive” and could make it worse, as automation displaces jobs and the poorest lose access to emerging technology, Schellekens warned.
“We must make sure that the marginalised users participate,” he said, pointing to Pakistani women as one large cohort already being sidelined, with their limited access to smartphones.
The report also outlines the tremendous potential of AI, from streamlining public services and bringing healthcare and education to remote areas, to improving job quality, climate analysis and economic growth.
But it cautions that without deliberate inclusion, the technology could deepen divides: hollowing out labour markets, concentrating wealth and leaving nations without access or ownership of the core technologies further behind.
AI could close gaps or widen them, depending on the choices made now
‘The Next Great Divergence’, UNDP report
“Human development is more than productivity and jobs,” the report said. “It is about opportunities, choices, freedoms and agency for people to live their life with dignity beyond wants and fear.”
While AI “can expand people’s freedoms and capabilities”, it warned that “new technologies rarely spread evenly. Moving faster than any technology before it, AI could close gaps or widen them, depending on the choices made now”.
Those choices will matter most in countries like Thailand, where 10 per cent of the population hold 70 per cent of national wealth.
For Thai AI trainer Prin Chamroenpanich, founder of Artificial & Emotional Intelligence of You (AEIO), the gap in AI literacy is already significant and growing fast.
“Thailand has high adoption, but when it comes to using AI to its full capacity, most people barely scratch the surface,” he told This Week in Asia.
“The real divide today isn’t between people who use AI and those who don’t. It’s between people who use AI at a basic, surface level and those who can leverage it to build businesses, drive revenue and scale their capabilities. This inequality gap will only grow.”
But it is not all doom and gloom, according to Yusuff Ismadi. The advertising art director in Kuala Lumpur believes AI does not necessarily have to destroy creative work.
“These days you’ll likely spend more time structuring the idea than crafting the visual to get your idea across,” the 29-year-old said. “It’s a great tool, but you’re not an artist just from using it. You just know how to use a brush.”

data.title: ‘Lesson not learned’: watchdog says fire risks persist on Hong Kong construction sites
data.description:
Fire safety risks still persist on construction sites across Hong Kong following the deadly blaze in Tai Po, the industry watchdog has warned, after a scaffolding worker in a major subsidised estate was sacked for smoking.
Thomas Ho On-sing, chairman of the Construction Industry Council, on Saturday criticised the industry for failing to “learn a lesson” from the Tai Po tragedy.
“I have been inspecting construction sites recently and found that many still have styrofoam boards and volatile paint. They also continue to use flammable materials for covering objects,” he told a radio programme.
“When they were removing scaffolding nets, they climbed up and down without safety belts. They have not learned a lesson from the pain.”
Ho said cigarette butts could still be found in dark corners and even in hazardous spots on construction sites.
“We must remove all potential sources of fire and flammable materials from construction sites, without any compromise,” he said.
The fire broke out at Wang Fuk Court of Tai Po on November 26, claiming at least 159 lives, including one firefighter and five construction workers. It also left an estimated 4,000 people homeless.

On Friday, Secretary for Labour and Welfare Chris Sun Yuk-han said authorities would discuss and work with the new Legislative Council to introduce a blanket ban on smoking at construction sites once it was elected.
Chau Sze-kit, chairman of the Hong Kong Construction Industry Employees General Union, said that most construction sites enforced their own smoking bans, with some designating specific areas for it.
But the practice created loopholes by allowing workers to bring in cigarettes, increasing the risk of them lighting up in unnoticed areas.
Fulam Construction Engineering, the contractor for renovation work at Sui Wo Court in Sha Tin, said earlier that it had dismissed a scaffolding worker who was caught smoking on the project site.
He was also permanently barred from working at Sui Wo Court sites.
The action followed earlier complaints from residents of Block J at the estate. The subsidised housing development comprises more than 3,500 households across nine blocks.
The company vowed to enhance patrolling and encourage workers to remind one another to maintain a safe working environment.
In response, Fulam offered a token of appreciation of HK$2,000 (US$257) to the residents, who requested that the company donate the amount to those affected by the fire at Wang Fuk Court.
While supporting the proposed statutory ban, Chau noted that public opinion had prematurely blamed workers’ smoking for the Tai Po fire before any investigation was conducted.
“This incident was not caused by a cigarette butt, but by a systemic failure involving site management, work arrangements, supervision of materials used and whether professionals and the property management company had fulfilled their duties,” he told the same programme.
Echoing his remarks, Ho said that when he visited family members of the five deceased workers in the blaze together with Chau, the pair were slammed by a victim’s daughter.
“She said her father never smoked, but some online comments were really mean, saying deaths served them right as workers have smoked,” Ho said.
“She questioned why we, as professionals and managers, had failed to provide a safe environment for workers. I don’t know how to respond to that,” he added. “What she wants is not money, but justice.”
Outgoing lawmaker Michael Tien Puk-sun, appearing on the same show, urged the government to step up prosecution to ensure deterrence.
“For [investigative outcomes], I will just look at the number of people to be held accountable and how they will be punished,” he said.
“With [prosecutions], people will be scared. If not, whatever rules are made, they will not abide by them.”
